title: Authentication Fundamentals
subtitle: Passwords, hashing, bcrypt

concepts.title: Never store passwords
concepts.body: If your database leaks, plaintext passwords become an identity market overnight. Store only a one-way hash.

concepts.title: Salts defeat rainbow tables
concepts.body: A unique salt per password means precomputed hash tables fail. Two identical passwords produce different hashes.

concepts.title: bcrypt work factor
concepts.body: The cost parameter forces attackers to spend real CPU per guess. Tune it so legitimate logins stay fast and attacks stay expensive.

practical.successNote: Scaffolding SecureAuth into the project root.

takeaways: Hash + salt, never encrypt passwords for storage
Work factors buy you time against brute force
SecureAuth init sets sane defaults so teams don't freestyle crypto

title: JWT & Sessions
subtitle: Tokens, expiry, refresh

concepts.title: Header · Payload · Signature
concepts.body: A JWT is three Base64URL segments. Anyone can read the payload — only the signature proves it wasn't tampered with.

concepts.title: exp is not optional
concepts.body: Long-lived tokens are stolen credentials with a delayed fuse. Short access tokens + refresh rotation limit blast radius.

concepts.title: Sessions vs JWT
concepts.body: Server sessions let you revoke instantly. JWTs scale horizontally but need careful expiry, denylist, or short TTL design.

practical.prompt: Decode this demo JWT. What is the subject (sub) claim?
practical.successNote: Payload is public — never put secrets in JWT claims.

takeaways: Payload is readable; signature is the trust boundary
Inspect tokens before you defend them

title: Rate Limiting & Account Lockout
subtitle: Brute-force defense

concepts.title: Volume is the attack
concepts.body: Credential stuffing doesn't need sophistication — it needs attempts. Cap attempts per identity and per IP.

concepts.title: Progressive slowdown
concepts.body: After each failure, increase delay. Attackers feel friction long before a hard lockout triggers support tickets.

concepts.title: Lockout with recovery
concepts.body: Hard lockouts stop floods. Pair them with safe unlock paths (time, email, admin) so real users aren't stranded.

practical.prompt: Simulate failed logins until lockout. How many failures trigger the lock?
practical.successNote: Progressive delay + hard lock = expensive guessing.

takeaways: Rate limit identity and network surfaces
Slowdown is a feature, not lag
Always design the unlock path with the lockout

title: OWASP Top 10: Injection & XSS
subtitle: Vulnerable vs fixed patterns

concepts.title: Injection = data becomes code
concepts.body: When user input is concatenated into a query, the attacker writes part of your program.

concepts.title: XSS = the browser trusts the page
concepts.body: Unencoded output in HTML/JS contexts lets attackers run script as your origin — cookies, tokens, and all.

concepts.title: Fix the boundary
concepts.body: Parameterized queries and context-aware encoding stop the class of bug. Frameworks help only if you don't bypass them.

practical.prompt: Which line number in the vulnerable snippet concatenates user input into SQL?
vulnerable: const q = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '" + id + "'";
fixed: const q = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?';
practical.successNote: Parameter binding keeps data out of the SQL grammar.

takeaways: Never string-build queries with user input
Encode for the output context (HTML, attr, JS)

title: Secure Deployment Basics
subtitle: Env vars, HTTPS, secure cookies

concepts.title: Secrets leave the repo
concepts.body: API keys and signing secrets belong in environment variables or a secrets manager — never in committed config.

concepts.title: HTTPS is table stakes
concepts.body: Without TLS, session cookies and tokens ride in cleartext. HSTS closes downgrade tricks.

concepts.title: Cookie flags
concepts.body: Secure + HttpOnly + SameSite reduce theft via network sniffing, XSS script access, and CSRF-style cross-site sends.

practical.prompt: Verify each go-live control. Check all items to complete.
items: Secrets loaded from environment (not source control)

takeaways: Ship with a review, not hope
